---
title: "Assessment Intelligence Plot Catalog"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-fold: false
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

```{r setup}
library(dplyr)
library(knitr)
library(readr)

find_repo_root <- function(start = getwd()) {
  current <- normalizePath(start, mustWork = TRUE)
  for (unused in seq_len(10)) {
    if (file.exists(file.path(current, "DESCRIPTION")) &&
        file.exists(file.path(current, "README.md"))) {
      return(current)
    }
    parent <- dirname(current)
    if (identical(parent, current)) {
      break
    }
    current <- parent
  }
  stop("Could not find repository root.", call. = FALSE)
}

repo_root <- find_repo_root()
repo_path <- function(...) file.path(repo_root, ...)
```

## Purpose

This catalog is organized around analytical decision questions in assessment
intelligence. It uses only synthetic response events, model outputs, and
assessment metrics. It does not use real student, parent, school, employer,
personnel, contact, job-search, private-note, credential, or `.env` data.

The first version is intentionally small: raincloud, hexbin, and calibration
plots. R is the primary implementation language because the repository is
already R-first. Python is omitted in this MVP because it would add dependency
surface without improving these three visuals.

Rebuild the catalog inputs and rendered plots from the repository root:

```bash
Rscript --vanilla R/plots/generate_plot_catalog_data.R
Rscript --vanilla R/plots/render_plot_catalog_plots.R
quarto render docs/plot_catalog.qmd --to html
```

## Raincloud Plot

**Analytical question**

Which support model changes the full score distribution, not just the mean?

**When to use it**

Use a raincloud plot when the decision depends on distribution shape, outliers,
spread, and summary statistics at the same time. In assessment intelligence, it
is useful for comparing intervention groups, form versions, scoring conditions,
or scoring policies.

**When not to use it**

Do not use it when there are too many groups to compare cleanly, when the sample
size is tiny, or when the decision is only about a single threshold rate. For
threshold questions, an ECDF or bar chart of rates is usually clearer.

**Synthetic data example**

```{r raincloud-data}
read_csv(repo_path("data/synthetic/plot_catalog_assessment_scores.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE) %>%
  select(response_id, support_model, assessment_window, score_percent, completion_time_minutes) %>%
  slice_head(n = 6) %>%
  kable()
```

**Rendered visual**

```{r raincloud-visual, out.width="100%"}
include_graphics(repo_path("outputs/plots/raincloud_assessment_distribution.png"))
```

**Brief interpretation**

The synthetic AI practice group has the highest center and a tighter lower tail
than the baseline review group. The baseline group has visibly more low-score
mass, which would matter if the decision question is about reducing fragile
mastery rather than simply raising the average.

**R code**

```{r raincloud-code, echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
source("R/plots/plot_raincloud.R")

plot_raincloud(
  data_path = "data/synthetic/plot_catalog_assessment_scores.csv",
  output_path = "outputs/plots/raincloud_assessment_distribution.png"
)
```

Reusable source: [`R/plots/plot_raincloud.R`](../R/plots/plot_raincloud.R)

**Portfolio relevance**

This plot shows assessment analytics judgment: the point is not a flashy
distribution chart, but a compact way to compare equity of outcomes, score
spread, and intervention effects with public-safe data.

## Hexbin Plot

**Analytical question**

Where is the dense relationship between readiness and growth hidden by
overplotting?

**When to use it**

Use a hexbin plot when there are thousands of assessment events and a scatterplot
would hide the actual data density. It is useful for readiness-growth
relationships, predicted-vs-observed patterns, timing-vs-performance patterns,
and other dense metric pairs.

**When not to use it**

Do not use it when individual observations must be audited one by one, when the
sample size is small enough for a scatterplot, or when the binning resolution
would change the interpretation. Binned density is a summary, not a substitute
for record-level review.

**Synthetic data example**

```{r hexbin-data}
read_csv(repo_path("data/synthetic/plot_catalog_dense_relationships.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE) %>%
  select(response_id, readiness_index, practice_completion_rate, growth_points, assessment_form) %>%
  slice_head(n = 6) %>%
  kable()
```

**Rendered visual**

```{r hexbin-visual, out.width="100%"}
include_graphics(repo_path("outputs/plots/hexbin_readiness_growth.png"))
```

**Brief interpretation**

The densest synthetic region sits around moderate-to-high readiness and positive
growth. The relationship is nonlinear: growth rises more sharply in the upper
readiness range. That pattern is useful for targeting follow-up modeling, but it
should not be read as causal from the plot alone.

**R code**

```{r hexbin-code, echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
source("R/plots/plot_hexbin.R")

plot_hexbin(
  data_path = "data/synthetic/plot_catalog_dense_relationships.csv",
  output_path = "outputs/plots/hexbin_readiness_growth.png"
)
```

Reusable source: [`R/plots/plot_hexbin.R`](../R/plots/plot_hexbin.R)

**Portfolio relevance**

This plot demonstrates practical learning analytics: dense assessment data often
needs aggregation before a decision-maker can see the signal. The implementation
uses a custom R hexbin routine, so the catalog does not require additional
plotting packages for this MVP.

## Calibration Plot

**Analytical question**

Can predicted mastery probabilities be trusted as probabilities?

**When to use it**

Use a calibration plot when an AI scorer, mastery model, or risk model outputs a
probability and the decision depends on the probability being honest. A model
with good ranking but bad calibration can still mislead threshold policies.

**When not to use it**

Do not use it as the only evaluation of a model. Calibration does not show
ranking quality, subgroup fairness, construct validity, or whether the target
itself is worth predicting. It also needs enough cases in each probability bin.

**Synthetic data example**

```{r calibration-data}
read_csv(repo_path("data/synthetic/plot_catalog_calibration.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE) %>%
  select(case_id, task_family, predicted_mastery_probability, observed_mastery) %>%
  slice_head(n = 6) %>%
  kable()
```

**Rendered visual**

```{r calibration-visual, out.width="80%"}
include_graphics(repo_path("outputs/plots/calibration_predicted_mastery.png"))
```

**Brief interpretation**

The synthetic model is close to calibrated in the middle of the probability
range, but high-probability bins fall below the dashed line. That indicates
overconfidence: predictions near 80-90% mastery are followed by lower observed
mastery rates.

**R code**

```{r calibration-code, echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
source("R/plots/plot_calibration.R")

plot_calibration(
  data_path = "data/synthetic/plot_catalog_calibration.csv",
  output_path = "outputs/plots/calibration_predicted_mastery.png"
)
```

Reusable source: [`R/plots/plot_calibration.R`](../R/plots/plot_calibration.R)

**Portfolio relevance**

This plot connects assessment analytics to AI evaluation. It shows that the
project can evaluate whether probabilistic claims are trustworthy, not merely
whether a model produces plausible-looking scores.

## Next Plots To Add

The next three additions should be:

1. ECDF plot for threshold and cumulative distribution questions.
2. Agreement or Bland-Altman plot for human-vs-AI scoring agreement.
3. Heatmap for skill-by-standard or model-by-task diagnostic patterns.

Those three extend the catalog into threshold policy, scorer agreement, and
diagnostic coverage without overbuilding the first release.
